meta.title: Package Management & Bundlers
meta.description: npm/yarn/pnpm package management, webpack/Vite bundler config, and dependency hygiene

items.text: Is the lockfile committed and consistent with package.json?

baseContent.whatItMeans: package-lock.json, yarn.lock, or pnpm-lock.yaml must be committed. It ensures everyone installs the exact same versions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a lockfile, npm install resolves versions differently on different machines, causing 'works on my machine' bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Verify the lockfile exists in the repo. Check it wasn't gitignored. Verify it's updated when package.json changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: The lockfile is gitignored. Commit it so all developers and CI use the same dependency versions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always commit lockfiles. They ensure reproducible installs across all environments.

items.text: Are version ranges appropriate (not too loose, not too strict)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use ^ (minor updates) for most deps. Pin exact versions for critical deps. Avoid * or > ranges.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Too loose (>=) allows breaking changes. Too strict (exact) prevents security patches. ^ is the right balance for most cases.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for * or >= ranges in package.json. Verify critical dependencies (auth, crypto) use exact versions.
baseContent.exampleComment: express: '>=4' allows major version bumps that could break the app. Use '^4.18.0' instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use ^ for most dependencies. Pin exact versions for security-critical packages.

items.text: Are new dependencies justified (not duplicating existing functionality)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before adding a dependency, check if the functionality exists in the language/framework or an already-installed package.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every dependency adds bundle size, attack surface, and maintenance burden. Left-pad taught us that micro-dependencies are risky.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the new dependency's functionality is already available. Evaluate the package's maintenance, size, and security history.
baseContent.exampleComment: is-even has 1 line of code: return n % 2 === 0. Don't add a dependency for this — write it inline.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Evaluate before adding dependencies. Consider: Is it maintained? How big is it? Can I write this myself?

items.text: Are devDependencies and dependencies correctly categorized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test frameworks, linters, build tools, and type definitions belong in devDependencies. Runtime deps go in dependencies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: devDependencies in dependencies bloats production images. Dependencies in devDependencies causes runtime crashes in production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test frameworks (jest, mocha) in dependencies. Check for runtime packages (express, react) in devDependencies.
baseContent.exampleComment: jest is in dependencies but should be in devDependencies. It bloats the production install by 30MB.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put runtime deps in dependencies, build/test tools in devDependencies. This matters for production installs.

items.text: Are known vulnerabilities in dependencies addressed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run npm audit / yarn audit regularly. Address critical and high vulnerabilities. Use Dependabot or Renovate for automated updates.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Known vulnerabilities in dependencies are the easiest attack vector. Automated scanners find and exploit them.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run npm audit. Check for critical/high vulnerabilities. Verify Dependabot or similar is configured.
baseContent.exampleComment: npm audit shows 3 critical vulnerabilities. Update the affected packages or evaluate if the vulnerability applies to your usage.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run dependency audits in CI. Address critical vulnerabilities promptly. Use automated update tools.

items.text: Are npm scripts clear and consistent (start, build, test, lint)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use standard script names: start, build, test, lint, format. Document custom scripts. Use pre/post hooks sparingly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-standard script names force developers to read package.json before doing anything. Standard names work with npm conventions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that npm start, npm test, and npm run build work. Verify custom scripts are documented.
baseContent.exampleComment: The build script is named 'compile'. Rename to 'build' to follow npm conventions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use standard npm script names. Document custom scripts in the README.

items.text: Is the Node.js version specified in engines?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set the engines field in package.json to specify the required Node.js version. Use .nvmrc or .node-version for automatic switching.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without engine specification, developers use different Node.js versions, causing subtle compatibility issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check package.json for engines field. Verify .nvmrc exists and matches the engines specification.
baseContent.exampleComment: No engines field specified. Add "engines": { "node": ">=20" } and create a .nvmrc with '20'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Specify Node.js version in engines and .nvmrc. Keeps all developers on the same version.

items.text: Are .env files gitignored with a .env.example template?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Never commit .env files with secrets. Provide .env.example with placeholder values for developer setup.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Committed .env files expose secrets (API keys, database passwords) to anyone with repo access. Secrets persist in git history.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check .gitignore for .env. Verify .env files aren't tracked in git. Check for .env.example with documented variables.
baseContent.exampleComment: .env is committed with real API keys. Add .env to .gitignore, rotate the exposed keys, and provide .env.example.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Gitignore .env files. Provide .env.example. Rotate any accidentally committed secrets.

items.text: Is code splitting configured for route-based or component-based lazy loading?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Split the bundle by route or heavy component using dynamic import() or framework-specific lazy loading.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single large bundle delays initial page load. Users download admin code they never visit. Code splitting loads only what's needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the bundle size. Verify large routes use dynamic imports. Check for vendor chunk separation.
baseContent.exampleComment: The entire admin panel (500KB) is in the main bundle. Use dynamic import() to lazy-load it only when visited.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Split by route at minimum. Lazy-load heavy components like charts, editors, and admin panels.

items.text: Are imports optimized for tree-shaking (named imports, not entire libraries)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Import specific functions, not entire libraries: import { debounce } from 'lodash-es' not import _ from 'lodash'.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Importing entire libraries includes unused code. import _ from 'lodash' adds 70KB. import { debounce } from 'lodash-es' adds 1KB.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for default imports of utility libraries (lodash, date-fns, rxjs). Verify named/path imports are used.
baseContent.exampleComment: import * as _ from 'lodash' includes the entire 70KB library. Use import { debounce } from 'lodash-es'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use named imports for tree-shaking. Use ESM versions of libraries (-es suffix).

items.text: Are source maps configured correctly for each environment?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use full source maps in development. Use hidden-source-map or no source maps in production (or upload to error tracking).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Source maps in production expose original source code. Missing source maps in development make debugging impossible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check devtool/sourcemap config. Verify production doesn't serve source maps publicly. Check if they're uploaded to Sentry/similar.
baseContent.exampleComment: Production serves full source maps. Use 'hidden-source-map' and upload them to Sentry for error tracking only.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Hide source maps in production. Upload them to error tracking services for debugging.

items.text: Has the bundle been analyzed for size bloat?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use webpack-bundle-analyzer, source-map-explorer, or similar to visualize bundle contents and find bloat.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hidden bloat (moment.js locales, unused polyfills, duplicate packages) can double bundle size without obvious cause.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run bundle analysis. Check for unexpectedly large packages, duplicate dependencies, and unused code.
baseContent.exampleComment: moment.js locales add 300KB. Switch to date-fns or dayjs, or configure webpack to exclude unused locales.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Analyze bundle size before releases. Watch for large dependencies and duplicates.

baseContent.whatItMeans: In monorepos, internal packages should use workspace: protocol. Shared deps should be hoisted. Version consistency matters.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong workspace configuration causes packages to use different versions of shared deps, creating subtle bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for workspace: references between packages. Verify shared dependencies have consistent versions across packages.
baseContent.exampleComment: Package A uses react 18.2 and package B uses react 18.1. They'll bundle different React versions. Align in the root.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use workspace: for internal deps. Hoist shared dependencies. Keep versions consistent.

items.text: Is the build order correct for interdependent packages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Packages that depend on other workspace packages must build after their dependencies. Use turborepo or nx for orchestration.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Building in the wrong order causes import failures. Package B depends on A's types, but A isn't built yet.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check build scripts for dependency-aware orchestration. Verify turborepo/nx pipeline configuration handles the build graph.
baseContent.exampleComment: The CI script builds all packages in parallel but package-b depends on package-a's types. Add build dependency in turbo.json.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use dependency-aware build tools (turborepo, nx). Don't rely on filesystem ordering.
